title: An approach to retarget the large serine recombinase Bxb1 enables precise integration of large DNA payloads at desired loci
summary: Frank Buchholz at TU Dresden reports a method to reprogram the large serine recombinase Bxb1 for programmable genome integration, enabling precise insertion of large DNA payloads at designated genomic loci. This work extends the utility of site-specific recombination as an alternative or complement to CRISPR-based approaches for delivering sizeable therapeutic transgenes that exceed the capacity of standard viral vectors. The approach was published in Nature Biotechnology (DOI 10.1038/s41587-026-03198-x, 2026). Broader implications for gene therapy include the ability to target defined genomic safe harbors with large multi-gene cassettes, which is relevant for complex pathway engineering and multigene replacement therapies. The research builds on a related companion paper by Fauser et al. in the same issue (10.1038/s41587-026-03186-1).
analysis_notes: Content is a paywall-gated Nature Biotechnology article preview only. No full abstract, methods, or results text available. Summary inferred from title, subject tags, reference list, and author affiliations. Priority set to medium due to limited evidence quality; the work appears to be a method paper on retargetable site-specific recombination relevant to gene therapy but concrete therapeutic claims or validation data could not be assessed.

title: A genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation atlas of human induced pluripotent stem cells
summary: The authors constructed an expressed, genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation cell atlas in KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs), mapping transcriptional phenotypes across 11,692 perturbed genes spanning over 2.5 million single cells. By correlating perturbed phenotypes, they reconstructed a cell map of the pluripotent state that faithfully recapitulated functionally related protein complexes. Leveraging the atlas, they identified the metabolic factor ZBTB41 and the pluripotency regulator RNF7, validating both through metabolic tracing, immunofluorescence, and protein-protein interaction assays. A complementary genome-wide screen of A-to-I RNA-editing modulators uncovered DBR1 as a potent regulator of transcriptome-wide RNA editing. The dataset is made publicly accessible via an interactive web browser (https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/), fastq files in the SRA under BioProject PRJNA1173491, and the analysis pipeline on GitHub.

No pharma/biotech companies or drug candidates mentioned; this is a fundamental resource paper. Priority reflects high-impact journal and scale of the dataset (11,692 genes, >2.5M cells) which has broad utility for stem cell biology and therapeutic pipeline development.

- [Induced pluripotent stem cells](https://www.nature.com/subjects/induced-pluripotent-stem-cells)

## Abstract

Comprehensively mapping the relationship between genotype and phenotype offers essential insights into how a cell’s state arises from its genetic components. Toward this goal, we generated an expressed genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation cell atlas in KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells, mapping transcriptional phenotypes associated with 11,692 perturbed genes across >2.5 million single cells. Using correlations among perturbed phenotypes, we created a cell map of the pluripotent state, demonstrating rich recapitulation of functionally related protein complexes. We then explored the atlas to uncover metabolic factor ZBTB41 and pluripotency regulator RNF7, validating their functions through metabolic tracing, immunofluorescence and protein–protein interaction assays. Lastly, we leveraged the atlas to generate a genome-scale screen of A-to-I RNA-editing modulators assayed through direct transcriptome-wide RNA editing, uncovering and mechanistically validating DBR1 as a potent regulator. Taken together, our data provide a comprehensive resource for interrogating the regulatory networks governing pluripotency, which is accessible at [https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J\_Perturbation\_Cell\_Atlas/](https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/).

### [Extended Data Fig. 1 Perturb-Seq of Chromatin Modifiers and Metabolic Enzymes in KOLF2.1J hiPSCs.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w/figures/5)

( **a**) Perturb-Seq pilot screens experimental scheme. KOLF2.1J iPSCs constitutively expressing KRABZIM3-dCas9 were transduced with a sgRNA library targeting two gene sets: (i) a panel of 108 chromatin modifying genes and (ii) a panel of 97 genes involved in metabolism. 108 non-targeting control (NTC) sgRNA were included. Following 2 day recovery, cells receiving perturbations were selected via puromycin for 2 days. On day 4, cells were harvested for single cell RNA sequencing. (Created using Biorender [https://BioRender.com/f8grmmb](https://biorender.com/f8grmmb)) ( **b-l**) correspond to results from the chromatin modifiers screen, while ( **m-q**) correspond to results from the metabolic enzymes screen. ( **b**) Number of cells per perturbation, indicating that 92% of perturbations (sgRNA) have more than 25 associated cells. ( **c**) On-target knockdown per perturbation, indicating that 65% of perturbations demonstrate repression >30%. ( **d**) Energy distance permutation testing of perturbations vs NTC cells. ( **e**) Relationship between energy distance and number of induced differentially expressed genes (DEGs) per perturbation. Perturbations with significance for both metrics are retained. sgRNA are combined per gene to form Strong Perturbations. ( **f**) Number of up- and down- regulated DEGs per Strong Perturbation, identified via DESeq2. ( **g**) Waterfall plot ranking Strong Perturbations on their average combined rank of energy distance and DEGs, with the top 10 labeled. ( **h**) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of strong perturbations. ( **i**) Exploration of alternate methods of clustering perturbations (See [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Sec8)). ( **j**) Annotated Minimum Distortion Embedding (MDE) of Strong Perturbation correlation matrix. ( **k**) UMAP density visualization of perturbed and NTC cells (top), DNMT1 and UHRF1 (bottom). ( **l**) Mechanism by which URHF1 recruits DNMT1 to regulate hemimethylated DNA. Modified from Arita et al.18 (Created using Biorender [https://BioRender.com/rvwc39u](https://biorender.com/rvwc39u)) ( **m, n**) Effect of loading variable number of cells per 10X channel on yield of cells with 1 sgRNA assigned for metabolic enzymes Perturb-Seq dataset. ( **o**) Energy distance permutation testing of perturbations vs NTC cells. ( **p**) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of Strong Perturbations. ( **q**) UMAP density visualization of perturbed and NTC cells (top), MTOR (bottom).

### [Extended Data Fig. 2 Data Analysis Pipeline PASTA (Perturbation Atlas Sanitization, Triage, and Analysis).](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w/figures/6)

Plots correspond to data from expressed genome wide Perturb-Seq assay. See [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Sec8) for full details. Data is first sanitized ( **a-f**), removing outlier cells and non-effective perturbations. Next, perturbations are triaged, removing low-priority perturbations that do not measurably cause cell state alterations ( **g-k**). Finally, analysis is performed to develop a cellular map. ( **l**, Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig2)). ( **a**) sgRNA are assigned per cell using 10x Genomics guide calling algorithm. Cells without exactly 1 sgRNA are filtered. ( **b**) Genes not detected in any single cells are removed. ( **c**) Cells are filtered based on standard QC metrics proposed by Heumos et al.[103](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR103 "Heumos, L. et al. Best practices for single-cell analysis across modalities. Nat. Rev. Genet. 24, 550–572 (2023).") ( **d**) Sanity check of standard QC metrics. ( **e**) Filtering of spurious NTC cells. NTC cells are batch-aligned via Harmony[105](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR105 "Korsunsky, I. et al. Fast, sensitive and accurate integration of single-cell data with Harmony. Nat. Methods 16, 1289–1296 (2019).") and filtered via Isolation Forest[109](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR109 "Liu, F. T., Ting, K. M. & Zhou, Z.-H. Isolation forest. In Proc. 8th IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (eds Giannotti, F. et al.) (IEEE, 2008).") assuming a 30% contamination fraction. ( **f**) Perturbations (sgRNA) with less than 25 associated cells and achieving less than 30% on-target knockdown are removed as proposed by Replogle et al.[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR36 "Replogle, J. M. et al. Mapping information-rich genotype-phenotype landscapes with genome-scale Perturb-Seq. Cell 185, 2559–2575 (2022).") ( **g**) Energy distance permutation test vs NTC via scPerturb[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#ref-CR15 "Peidli, S. et al. scPerturb: harmonized single-cell perturbation data. Nat. Methods 21, 531–540 (2024).") ( **h**) (Left) Differential expression test of perturbations vs NTC cells via DESeq2. (Right) Distribution of number of DEGs for each NTC sgRNA vs. all other NTC cells is used to set a FDR on the # of DEGs identified. ( **i**) Perturbations with both a significant energy distance test result and more DEGs than the NTC FDR baseline are selected. Perturbations are then combined per targeted gene. ( **j, k**) Energy distance testing and # of DEGs filtering is repeated at the gene level. ‘Strong Perturbations’ are defined as those which pass all filtering from steps a-k. ( **l**) Strong Perturbations are prioritized based on the average of their energy distance and # of DEGs rank.

### [Extended Data Fig. 3 Further Analysis Related to CRISPRi Fitness Screening.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w/figures/7)

( **a**) Top 5 GO terms for enriched/depleted perturbations related to Fig. [1(c)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1). ( **b**) Fitness screen analysis Z-normalizing to distribution of NTC sgRNA rather than all perturbations (related to Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **c**) Fitness screen analysis Z-normalizing to distribution of NTC sgRNA rather than all perturbations displays the same pathways as when Z-normalizing to all (related to Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **c**) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of DepMap exclusive essential genes (related to Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **e**) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of essential genes shared between DepMap and KOLF2.1Js (related to Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03199-w#Fig1)). ( **f, g**) Fitness Z-score at Day 6 (D6) and Day 14 (D14) in KOLF2.1J KRABZIM3-dCas9 expressing cells at the perturbation level. Each dot represents a different gRNA, 3 gRNA/perturbation and 478 gRNAs for NTC conditions. Red bars are perturbations with z-score < −1 at day 14, blue bars are perturbations with z-score>1 at day 14. title: Editor’s Pick: Trogenix
summary: Nature Biotechnology names Trogenix in its annual “editor’s pick” listing of companies that secured sizeable early-stage funding in 2025. The Philadelphia-based company has developed a platform for generating “synthetic super-enhancers,” synthetic regulatory DNA elements designed to drive highly cell-specific gene expression. This technology could enable precision gene therapy targeting of solid tumors, including glioblastoma, the most aggressive form of brain cancer. The selection signals recognition of Trogenix’s platform as having significant scientific and commercial potential in the rapidly expanding field of gene therapy and synthetic biology-based therapeutics.
analysis_notes: Article content is extremely thin—only a single paragraph. Nature Biotechnology's annual "Editor's Pick" series features brief profiles of well-funded startups. The paywall prevented access to the full article text, so this summary is based on the visible excerpt only. Key detail: Trogenix develops synthetic super-enhancers for cell-specific gene therapy in solid tumors (glioblastoma mentioned as concrete therapeutic indication). No specific funding amount is stated.

title: Editor's Pick: Liberate Bio
summary: Nature Biotechnology has named Liberate Bio as one of the standout companies to watch, highlighting its receipt of sizeable early-stage funding in 2025. The Berlin-based company is building a lipid nanoparticle (LNP) toolbox designed to deliver genetic therapeutics to cell types inside the body that have historically been inaccessible to LNP-mediated delivery. This positions Liberate Bio as part of an increasingly competitive landscape of RNA and gene therapy delivery platform companies, where unlocking new tissue targets is the keyDifferentiator. The profile signals that Nature Biotechnology sees meaningful potential in the company's approach to overcoming current LNP delivery limitations, which have long constrained the therapeutic scope of mRNA, siRNA, and other genetic medicines.
analysis_notes: Content quality is thin -- this is a brief Nature editorial profile rather than a full article. The full company details, funding amount, leadership team, and technical specifics are behind the paywall/login wall. Only one paragraph of content was accessible before the login prompt. For deeper due diligence, access through an institutional subscription or check Liberate Bio's own website/press releases for specific terms.

title: Mapping the spatial landscape of extracellular vesicles in tissues with Spatial-EV-seq
summary: Spatial-EV-seq is a newly developed method enabling spatially resolved profiling of extracellular vesicles (EVs) while preserving their native distribution within tissues. The technique integrates EV mapping with spatial transcriptomics to enable location-specific analysis of EVs and their communication networks within the tissue microenvironment. Published in Nature Biotechnology on 26 June 2026 by Wen, Q. et al., this approach establishes a new paradigm in spatial EV biology by providing the ability to map where specific EVs originate and interact within complex tissue architecture. For biotech operators and investors, this method represents a significant technical advance that could accelerate drug discovery programs targeting EV-mediated cell-cell communication pathways and enable more precise biomarker development from tissue-based diagnostics.

Spatial-EV-seq is a method for spatially resolved profiling of extracellular vesicles (EVs) in tissues while preserving EVs’ native distribution. By integrating EV mapping with spatial transcriptomics, Spatial-EV-seq enables location-specific analysis of EVs and their communication networks within the tissue microenvironment, establishing a new paradigm in the field of spatial EV biology.

title: Affordable centimeter-scale 3D microscopy with submicrometer resolution
summary: A Nature Biotechnology news/views piece summarizing work by Gong et al. on HySIL (hybrid solid-liquid optics), a refractive framework that achieves submicrometer-resolution 3D imaging of centimeter-scale biological samples. HySIL uses index-matched components to enable high-quality imaging across various immersion media using inexpensive air objectives, addressing long-standing constraints from short working distances, high cost, and inflexible design of immersion objectives. This matters for biotech operators conducting spatial transcriptomics, whole-organ imaging, and large-volume cleared tissue analysis — the technology promises lower-cost alternatives to traditional high-end microscopy platforms while maintaining resolution at scales previously only achievable with specialized (and expensive) immersion optics.
analysis_notes: This is a news/views summary article, not the primary research paper. Full text is behind the Nature paywall; only the abstract and references are visible. The actual research paper (DOI 10.1038/s41587-026-03172-7) by Gong et al. contains the detailed experimental results.

Submicrometer-resolution three-dimensional (3D) imaging of large samples has been constrained by the short working distance, high cost and inflexible design of immersion objectives. We developed hybrid solid–liquid optics (HySIL) — a refractive framework with index-matched components — for submicrometer-resolution 3D imaging of centimeter-scale samples in various immersion media using inexpensive air objectives.

title: Frontier AI companies as biotech acquirers
summary: Amelia Palermo examines the emergence of frontier AI and big tech companies as a new class of biotech buyer, contrasting their acquisition strategies with traditional pharmaceutical exits. The article is anchored by Anthropic's April 2026 acquisition of stealth AI startup Coefficient Bio for approximately $400 million, following Anthropics $30 billion Series G raise at a $380 billion post-money valuation earlier that year. Broader industry context includes OpenAIs launch of GPT-Rosalind for life sciences research and the NVIDIA-Lilly co-innovation AI lab partnership aimed at reinventing drug discovery through AI. Additional referenced activity spans Isomorphic Labs multi-target collaboration with Eli Lilly, suggesting a broader pattern of big tech absorbing foundational biology platforms rather than developing late-stage pipelines in-house. The piece signals implications for founders evaluating acquirer options beyond pharma and for investors assessing competition for early-stage AI-bio assets.
analysis_notes: Full article text is behind a Nature paywall; analysis derived from title, article metadata, abstract teaser paragraph, and reference list. The references themselves are informative for reconstructing the article's core arguments but should be treated as reconstructed evidence rather than direct quotes from the full text.

title: Author Correction: Quantification and transcriptome profiling reveal abundant, dynamic and translatable dephospho-CoA-capped RNAs
summary: This is an author correction published 16 June 2026 for a research article originally published online on 4 March 2026 in Nature Biotechnology. The correction addresses incorrect data in Extended Data Fig. 2i that resulted from a transcription error during article preparation, first spotted by the authors. The corrected figure and Source Data have been amended in both HTML and PDF versions. The authors -- led by Hao Hu and Xuemei Chen at Peking University's BEACON center -- state explicitly that the data errors do not affect the overall results or conclusions of the parent study.
analysis_notes: This is an author correction notice only, not the primary research article. The original paper (doi: 10.1038/s41587-026-03040-4) concerns dephospho-CoA-capped RNA biology and transcriptome profiling; no experimental results, drug candidates, or commercial developments are described in this correction document.

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03040-4](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03040-4), published online 4 March 2026.

In the version of this article initially published, due to a transcription error during article preparation, data for Extended Data Fig. 2i were incorrect. The errors do not affect the results or conclusions of the article. The figure and Source Data are now amended in the HTML and PDF versions of the article.

title: Author Correction: Generation of modified cows and sheep from spermatid-like haploid embryonic stem cells
summary: This is an author correction (published 25 June 2026) for a paper originally published in Nature Biotechnology on 7 October 2025. The correction addresses a figure preparation error in Fig. 3d: the bottom-right immunohistochemistry image incorrectly showed a sheep-sheep chimera and has been replaced with the correct cow-cow chimera image as it appears in the corrected HTML and PDF versions. The original study reported generation of modified cows and sheep from spermatid-like haploid embryonic stem cells by Lei Yang, Shaorong Gao, Guangpeng Li, and colleagues at Inner Mongolia University, Tongji University, and Northeast Agricultural University. The author note identifies deceased co-author Li Zhang.
analysis_notes: This is an author correction (not primary research) correcting a figure error in the original paper. It contains no new scientific data, commercial deals, drug trials, or investment signals — purely a publication integrity note about swapping one chimera image for another in supplementary figures.

title: Unlocking the chemical potential of filamentous fungi using prime editing
summary: Researchers have developed fPE7max, a prime editor optimized specifically for filamentous fungi that enables precise genome engineering ranging from single-base substitutions to kilobase-scale insertions and multi-kilobase deletions across diverse fungal species. The team applied this tool to edit translation-regulatory elements in upstream open reading frames (uORFs) controlling a pleiotropic metabolic regulator, which unlocked previously inaccessible biosynthetic gene clusters. This metabolic activation approach led to the discovery of new fungal metabolites that were not detectable under normal conditions. The work builds on prior prime editing advances (including original CRISPRprime editing from Anzalone 2019 and La protein optimizations from Yan 2024) and extends them to a eukaryotic organism context where base editors have struggled. For biotech operators, the methodology offers a generalizable platform for unlocking silent natural product biosynthetic pathways in industrially relevant filamentous fungi such as Aspergillus and Trichoderma species.
analysis_notes: Content is a subscription preview with limited body text — only the first two paragraphs were accessible. Title and entities were confirmed from DOI metadata and 'This is a summary of' line citing Sun et al. Nat Biotechnol 2026 (doi: 10.1038/s41587-026-03202-4). Details about specific fungi species, metabolite classes, or quantitative editing efficiencies are missing from the accessible text. Priority is high due to the strategic relevance of prime editing in filamentous fungi for natural product discovery and strain engineering, but a substantive update after full-text access would improve this assessment.

title: Publisher Correction: Lung and liver editing by lipid nanoparticle delivery of a stable CRISPR–Cas9 ribonucleoprotein
summary: This is a publisher correction published in Nature Biotechnology (June 18, 2026) for an original article originally released October 16, 2024 — 'Lung and liver editing by lipid nanoparticle delivery of a stable CRISPR–Cas9 ribonucleoprotein.' The correction addresses a single methodological text error in the Materials and Methods section under 'Directed evolution of GeoCas9': the phrase referring to '750 μl of sulfur-oxidizing bacteria' was corrected to read '750 μl of SOB medium (Super Optimal Broth).' The original research, led by Kai Chen, Hesong Han, Jennifer A. Doudna (HHMI and Gladstone Institutes), and Niren Murthy at UC Berkeley's Innovative Genomics Institute and Department of Molecular and Cell Biology, describes CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in lung and liver tissues via lipid nanoparticle delivery along with directed evolution work on the GeoCas9 nuclease. This correction does not alter any findings, data, or conclusions in the original paper; it only fixes a reagent description in the methods.
analysis_notes: This is a publisher correction, not a research article. The sole change corrects a methods reagent name (sulfur-oxidizing bacteria → SOB medium). No new data, endpoints, or biotech market implications are presented in this corrigendum. The underlying original paper on LNP-delivered CRISPR-Cas9 lung/liver editing may carry higher relevance for operators and investors.

title: Quantum computing in transition
summary: A Nature Biotechnology commentary argues that quantum advantage in biotechnology will emerge from hybrid quantum-classical systems rather than fully fault-tolerant quantum machines. Researchers have already encoded the entire hepatitis D virus genome on IBM quantum hardware, and results from the Q4Bio challenge demonstrated molecular simulations of protein complexes exceeding 12,000 atoms — the largest known for biologically meaningful molecules — using hybrid methods. IBM has predicted 2026 as the inflection point for true quantum advantage via hybrid platforms, while Google's Willow chip has achieved sub-threshold error rates needed for scalable fault tolerance, potentially realizable by 2030. The article raises urgent data security concerns: sensitive genomic and health records face a harvest-now-decrypt-later threat as RSA-2048 becomes increasingly vulnerable to quantum algorithms. It warns that early access will concentrate among large pharma, elite U.S./European/Chinese institutions and national labs, risking a quantum divide that leaves neglected diseases and low-income settings behind unless proactive global initiatives like UNESCO's proposed open-platform framework intervene.

Quantum computing in transition


[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x.pdf)

Hybrid quantum–classical computers will achieve quantum advantage in biotechnology, bridging the time until a full quantum computer becomes available.

The year 2025 was declared the International Year of Quantum Science and Technology by the United Nations General Assembly, celebrating 100 years of quantum science. Yet, amid this excitement surrounding quantum computers and their promise to revolutionize medicine, chemistry and biotechnology across the globe, an important reality is often overlooked: quantum advantage — the state in which a quantum computer solves a practical, real-world problem faster or more efficiently than the most powerful classical supercomputer — is unlikely to emerge from a fully quantum machine at first. It will instead come from hybrid quantum–classical computing platforms that combine the strengths of quantum processors with the scale and reliability of classical computing.

In fact, there have already been exciting developments in the application of quantum computing to biotechnology achieved by hybrid quantum–classical systems. Researchers have successfully encoded and analyzed the entire genome of the hepatitis D virus on IBM quantum hardware[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR1 "Offord, C. Science                  https://doi.org/10.1126/science.z9ookm7                                 (2026)."). Results from the international Quantum for Bio ( [Q4Bio](https://wellcomeleap.org/q4bio/)) challenge showed that hybrid quantum–classical methods can contribute to molecular simulation and drug-discovery problems, including simulation of protein complexes over 12,000 atoms — the largest known simulations of biologically meaningful molecules[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR2 "Merz, K. M. et al. Preprint at arXiv                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2605.01138                                 (2026)."). In January this year, IBM predicted that 2026 will be the inflection point where we will see a true quantum advantage using hybrid systems.

Current quantum computers are powerful but incomplete tools, and biological systems are complex. Quantum computers are not yet able to address biological challenges independently and at scale. Qubits, the fundamental unit of information in a quantum computer, are error-prone — vibrations or slight variations in temperature or electromagnetic field can cause loss of coherence. Current quantum computers are noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices: they have dozens or a few hundred qubits but are not error-corrected and introduce errors into their operations.

Hybrid systems use quantum error mitigation, reducing the impact of noise on quantum computations without requiring full error correction. Classical computers and quantum computers are tightly coupled in iterative loops where only small parts of the computation are performed on the quantum hardware and the classical computer is used to handle errors, perform optimization and process data. These systems act as a bridge to achieving quantum advantage, but they are not likely to scale well to the number of qubits required for use cases that are projected to eventually benefit most from quantum computing, such as molecular simulation and drug design, or protein or RNA folding.

Error correction techniques, by contrast, achieve physical correction of quantum states while maintaining the phase relationship between quantum states. Combining multiple physical qubits into a logical qubit detects and corrects errors without directly measuring the qubits’ states. However, a critical challenge here is that the correction itself adds more qubits and more error-prone operations to the system; therefore, the base error rate needs to be below a certain threshold to make error correction advantageous. Google’s Willow chip achieves this sub-threshold error rate[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR3 "Google Quantum AI and Collaborators. Nature                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-08449-y                                 (2025)."). As more qubits are added with this type of correction, the logical error rate is suppressed exponentially — hinting at possible routes to building a large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer, with estimates for realization as soon as 2030.

While researchers pursue the long-term goal of fault-tolerant quantum computers, the pharmaceutical industry has adopted a more pragmatic perspective, extracting useful insights from the quantum technologies available now and investing heavily in collaborations with quantum hardware providers so as to get ready to make use of a full-scale quantum computer once it becomes available. As these companies have access to sensitive intellectual property and clinical data, they must also consider the security risk. Sensitive encrypted data can be intercepted and stored using a ‘harvest now, decrypt later’ strategy, and once a sufficiently powerful quantum computer becomes available it will be able to be deciphered. Resource estimates for breaking RSA-2048, a commonly used encryption scheme, have dropped from around 20 million physical qubits to potentially fewer than 100,000 with new algorithms and architectures[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR4 "Cain et al. Preprint at arXiv                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.28627                                 (2026)."), [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR5 "Babbush et al. Preprint at arXiv                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.28846                                 (2026)"). Genomic and health data do not expire and are uniquely exposed to this threat: while the data themselves are often available, they are anonymized and the information needed to deanonymize them is encrypted. Data collected today and decrypted in 2035 will be as sensitive as on the day they were created — maybe more so, given that genomic analysis techniques will be more advanced. Post-quantum cryptographic standards have been available[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03233-x#ref-CR6 "National Institute of Standards and Technology. Federal Register                  https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/08/14/2024-17956/announcing-issuance-of-federal-information-processing-standards-fips-fips-203-module-lattice-based                                 (2024).") since 2024, introducing encryption methods that are designed to be secure against attacks by future quantum computers. Biological databases need to develop timelines for adoption to protect sensitive data.

Biotechnology already occupies a central role in economic competitiveness, healthcare innovation and national security. As these sectors converge in the realm of quantum computing, policymakers will face questions concerning infrastructure, workforce development, intellectual property and equitable access. Early engagement will be essential to ensure that scientific opportunity translates into societal benefit.

Technologies that are strategically sensitive and capital-intensive tend to concentrate in a limited number of hands, raising the possibility that quantum advantage may not be evenly distributed across the global scientific and industrial landscape. The institutions with early access — a handful of large pharmaceutical companies, national labs and well-funded universities in the United States, Europe and China — will gain first-mover advantages in identification of structurally difficult drug target identification, for example. This means that the first wave of quantum-enabled drug discoveries will almost certainly address diseases in areas where the institutions with quantum access have financial incentives to direct their computing. Neglected tropical diseases and antimicrobial resistance in low-income settings will not be early beneficiaries of quantum-accelerated biology. This is not an inevitable outcome, but it is the default trajectory unless explicitly countered by global initiatives such as the [one proposed by UNESCO](https://www.unesco.org/en/global-quantum-initiative), which aims to address this ‘quantum divide’ by providing an open platform for dialog, cooperation and capacity building.

Quantum computing is unlikely to transform biotechnology in a single leap. Instead, its impact is emerging through hybrid systems that combine quantum and classical strengths, delivering incremental advantage while full fault tolerance remains out of reach. The challenge is not only to build quantum advantage, but to ensure it arrives in a form that can be securely, responsibly and equitably used.

title: Author Correction: Improved RNA base editing with guide RNAs mimicking highly edited endogenous ADAR substrates
summary: This is an Author Correction to a paper published in Nature Biotechnology on 3 April 2025 by researchers at Sun Yat-Sen University and RecoRNA Biotechnology. The correction addresses a figure assembly error in the original article's Fig. 5a, where A549 cell line data was erroneously duplicated from U2OS data. The corrected figures have been updated in both HTML and PDF versions of the article. The underlying research demonstrated improved RNA base editing by engineering guide RNAs that mimic the sequence features of highly edited endogenous ADAR (adenosine deaminase acting on RNA) substrates, with potential implications for therapeutic RNA editing approaches. This correction does not alter the substantive scientific findings or conclusions of the original paper.
analysis_notes: This is an Author Correction, not the original research article. It reports a minor figure data error (A549 data duplicated from U2OS) that has been corrected. The substantive content on improved RNA base editing with ADAR-mimicking guide RNAs resides in the original paper (doi: 10.1038/s41587-025-02628-6). The correction itself contains no new scientific findings, trial data, or commercial developments.

title: Author Correction: Sustained nitric oxide production by engineered E. coli remodels the tumor microenvironment and potentiates immunotherapy
summary: Nature Biotechnology published an author correction on 6 July 2026 addressing data errors in the companion article (doi: 10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y) about engineered Escherichia coli designed to produce sustained nitric oxide within tumors. The correction identifies mistakes across multiple figure source datasets: an erroneously included anti-EGFR dataset in Fig. 4n, incorrect PBS group measurements in Fig. 6d that affected related plots, data entry errors in Figs. 4o and 5b-d/h, and inconsistent decimal-place formatting in Figs. 1a-b. The source data figures have been revised in both the HTML and PDF versions. All authors are from Peking University Third Hospital in Beijing with one contributor from the Agency for Science, Technology and Research in Singapore.

entities.name: Agency for Science, Technology and Research

analysis_notes: This is an author correction notice rather than a primary research article. It does not present new experimental findings; instead it corrects data errors in figures of the companion original article (doi 10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y). The corrected findings reportedly concern engineered E. coli producing nitric oxide to remodel tumor microenvironment and enhance immunotherapy efficacy, but no mechanistic detail, trial data, or commercial relevance is derivable from this correction alone.

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03054-y), published online 18 March 2026.

In the version of this article initially published, there was an error in the Fig. 4n Source Data, where an additional “anti-EGFR” dataset was accidentally included; an error in the Fig. 6d Source Data, with errors in the PBS group measurements, also affecting the plots in the article; errors in the Figs. 4o, 5b–d, h Source Data, due to data entry errors; and errors in the Fig. 1a, b Source Data, where decimal-place formatting was inconsistent.
